Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Julien Offray de La Mettrie

1709 – 1751

Materialism, embodiment, and the machine that feels and thinks.

La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a medical, empiricist, monist, anti-Cartesian, graded, embodied, affective, and cautiously theological natural history of mind. It is not merely the slogan “man is a machine.” That slogan is real and central, but misleading when isolated. His “machine” is not a dead clockwork artifact. It is a living, self-moving, sensitive, organized body. His “soul” is not simply denied; rather, the immaterial, separable, Cartesian-scholastic soul is denied, while the phenomena traditionally assigned to soul—sensation, thought, imagination, memory, passion, will, remorse, pleasure, intelligence—are retained and relocated within organized living matter.

A rigorous extraction of La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind gives us something like this: mind is the activity of organized sensibility in living matter, concentrated especially in the brain and nervous system, but dependent on the whole body, its nutrition, disease states, climate, education, passions, needs, pleasures, and inherited constitution. Human mentality differs from animal mentality not by an absolute metaphysical gulf, but by degree, complexity, organization, and acquired development. Plants occupy a lower limit of the same natural scale. Angels and God, when La Mettrie invokes them, should not be treated as disposable rhetorical ornaments. They mark the possible upper limit of a scalar metaphysics of minds, while also indicating the point at which empirical philosophy yields to faith, analogy, and epistemic humility.

The core difficulty is that La Mettrie is at once a radical materialist and a careful anti-dogmatist. He argues that the body appears to think, that all known mental operations depend on organization, brain, nerves, senses, and bodily condition; yet he repeatedly says that we do not know the essence of matter, the essence of soul, or the ultimate “how” by which matter thinks. That tension is not accidental. It is the operating structure of his philosophy.

⸻

1. Corpus and method: physician-philosophy rather than armchair metaphysics

The main texts for reconstructing La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind are Histoire naturelle de l’âme, later revised as Traité de l’âme; L’Homme machine; L’Homme plante; Les animaux plus que machines; Système d’Épicure; and the Discours préliminaire to his philosophical works. The 1774 Berlin Œuvres philosophiques explicitly gathers these texts and identifies the themes of mind/body, materialism, physiology, and the soul, while the table of contents of the Traité de l’âme shows the systematic breadth of his treatment: matter, motor power, the sensitive faculty of matter, memory, imagination, passions, instinct, sleep, dreams, intellectual faculties, and the relation between philosophy and faith.

La Mettrie’s decisive methodological move is to subordinate metaphysics to physiology. In L’Homme machine, he opens by contrasting systems about the soul and quickly reduces the major alternatives to spiritualism and materialism. He criticizes inherited metaphysical systems, including Cartesian dualism, Leibnizian monadology, and abstract speculation about the soul, and then insists that the human being must be studied by experience and observation. The privileged observers are not scholastic metaphysicians but physicians, because they see the soul altered, diminished, excited, or deranged by bodily states.

This makes La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind a form of medical empiricism. He repeatedly argues that knowledge of human nature must be gained a posteriori, through the organs. The soul is not something we first define in abstraction and then attach to the body. Rather, what we call soul is encountered through sleep, fever, digestion, opium, coffee, illness, brain injury, education, memory, imagination, and passions. The physician’s clinic replaces the metaphysician’s study.

This does not mean La Mettrie is a naïve empiricist who thinks observation immediately solves everything. On the contrary, one of his deepest claims is that experience gives strong grounds for materialism while still leaving ultimate essence obscure. We can know that mental life depends on bodily organization without knowing the final essence of matter or soul.

⸻

2. The central anti-Cartesian move

La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind is intelligible only against Descartes. Descartes had sharply divided extended body from thinking soul. He also treated non-human animals as machines. La Mettrie radicalizes and transforms this picture in two directions at once.

First, he extends machinery upward: human beings too are machines. Second, he transforms the meaning of machine: the living machine is not inert extension pushed from outside but organized, moving, sensitive matter. So La Mettrie is not simply a Cartesian mechanist who removes the soul. He is a critic of Cartesian mechanism from within: Descartes made animals too dead and humans too metaphysically exceptional. La Mettrie makes animals more alive and humans less metaphysically separate.

French scholarship has emphasized this point. Ann Thomson argues that the title L’Homme machine has encouraged readers to reduce La Mettrie to a simple Cartesian mechanical materialist, but that his thought is better situated in the problem of brain functioning and living organization than in a crude clockwork model. A later French interpretation similarly stresses that the “animal in man” is more than machine in the narrow sense: La Mettrie does not simply erase the soul but pluralizes, naturalizes, and disperses it into material interiority, bodily relations, and affective life.

So the basic anti-Cartesian formula is this: there are not two substances, body and soul; there is one living organization whose operations are bodily and mental at once.

⸻

3. Matter: not inert extension, but moving and sensitive

La Mettrie’s materialism is not the doctrine that reality is made of passive geometrical extension. He explicitly rejects the Cartesian reduction of matter to extension. In the Traité de l’âme, he says that the essence of matter is unknown and that the properties we observe include not only forms and extension but also motor force and the faculty of sensing.

This is crucial. If matter were merely inert extension, then thought would indeed seem impossible without an immaterial supplement. La Mettrie’s strategy is to deny the premise. Matter is not known to us as a dead substrate. It appears in nature as organized, moving, sensitive, transformable, and capable of increasingly complex operations. In the Traité, he treats motor power and sensibility as attributes or powers of matter, and he criticizes Cartesians for stripping matter of sensation and thereby producing the absurd doctrine that animals are pure machines without inner life.

This gives La Mettrie a distinctive position. He is not a dualist; he does not posit a spiritual soul as a second substance. But neither is he a reductive mechanist in the narrow seventeenth-century sense. His matter is closer to what one might call dynamic, organized, sentient matter. The soul is not added to matter from outside; the soul is what organized matter does when it reaches certain levels of complexity and sensitivity.

In L’Homme machine, this culminates in the claim that there is only one substance in the universe, diversely modified, and that the human being is the most perfect known modification of that substance. Yet this ontological monism remains tied to a repeated epistemic modesty: La Mettrie does not pretend to know the hidden essence of matter. Italian reception has rightly emphasized that his materialism is not a grand totalizing metaphysics of “matter in itself,” but an experimental, medical, and anti-theological account of the body as flesh, pleasure, pain, death, and thought.

⸻

4. What is the soul?

La Mettrie’s answer varies by context, but the systematic reconstruction is clear.

In the Traité de l’âme, he begins from ignorance. Aristotle, Plato, Descartes, and Malebranche do not teach us what the soul is; the essence of the soul of humans and animals is as unknown as the essence of matter and bodies. Soul and body are “made together,” so to speak; they appear together in the living being.

In L’Homme machine, he states the more radical version: all the faculties of the soul depend so much on the proper organization of the brain and body that the soul becomes a “vain term” if it is taken to name a separate being. Properly used, “soul” designates the thinking part in us, not an immaterial substance detached from the organism.

The most precise formulation is therefore:

The soul is not a separable substance. It is the organized, sensitive, thinking activity of the living body, especially the brain and nervous system.

La Mettrie’s language permits three registers.

The first is polemical: “soul” is a dangerous or empty term when it supports spiritualist dualism.

The second is phenomenological: “soul” names real phenomena—sensation, imagination, memory, passion, pleasure, judgment, remorse, attention, and thought.

The third is physiological: these phenomena are functions of organized matter, especially brain, nerves, senses, and bodily constitution.

This is why it would be misleading to say that La Mettrie simply “denies the soul.” He denies a certain metaphysical interpretation of the soul. He does not deny mental life. His project is to show that mental life is immanent in organized nature.

⸻

5. Brain, nerves, and bodily organization

La Mettrie’s theory of mind is brain-centered but not narrowly brain-isolated. The brain is privileged because it is the organ in which sensation, imagination, memory, and thought are coordinated; but the whole body affects the mind.

In L’Homme machine, he uses comparative anatomy to argue that human intelligence correlates with the structure, size, convolutions, and quality of the brain. He compares humans with quadrupeds, notes similarities in brain organization, and emphasizes that defects in brain organization produce imbecility, madness, or cognitive limitation.

He then broadens the evidence. Disease changes the soul. Sleep suspends or diminishes it. Food, digestion, temperature, drugs, age, fever, blood, nerves, and sensory organs alter thought and character. He remarks that body and soul sleep together, that nutrition can alter temperament and mind, and that everything depends on how the “machine” is tuned.

This is not merely a one-way causal reduction from body to mind. La Mettrie also observes that imagination, fear, desire, pain, and abstract thought can heat the blood, tense the nerves, produce feverish states, or alter bodily functioning. But he does not infer from this reciprocity that there are two substances. Instead, he asks why we should make double what is one. Will, sensation, imagination, and bodily motion are different operations of the same organized living machine.

This yields a deeply embodied philosophy of mind. Mind is not merely “in the head” in the modern computational sense. It is brain-centered, but the brain is embedded in digestion, blood, nerves, sexual constitution, climate, diet, illness, and social formation. The soul is not housed in the body like a pilot in a ship. It is the body’s own organized sensitivity.

⸻

6. Sensation as the root of thought

The foundational mental power for La Mettrie is sentiment, meaning sensation, feeling, or sensibility. Thought is not an independent faculty floating above sensation; it is a development of sensation.

In L’Homme machine, he explicitly says that movement and sensation excite one another, that thought is a faculty of feeling, and that the rational soul is the sensitive soul applied to the contemplation of ideas and to reasoning. When feeling is extinguished, thought is extinguished.

The Traité de l’âme says the same in more systematic terms. All intellectual faculties are contained in the faculty of feeling; without sensation, the soul would perform none of its functions.

This is one of La Mettrie’s most important claims. He is not merely saying that sensation provides raw material for thought, as in a moderate empiricism. He is saying that thought itself is a transformation of sensibility. Reason is not the sovereign opposite of sense. It is sense organized, retained, compared, intensified, and redirected.

That position has several consequences.

First, there is no pure intellect in the human being, if by pure intellect one means a faculty completely detached from bodily sensibility.

Second, intellectual hierarchy becomes a hierarchy of organization. The difference between an ordinary person and a genius is not a metaphysical difference of substance but a difference in organic constitution, education, memory, imagination, nervous delicacy, and the arrangement of the brain.

Third, animals cannot be excluded from mentality simply because they lack human language or abstract reason. If sensation is already the root of mind, animals have minds in the relevant sense.

Fourth, the theology of rational soul is displaced but not simply mocked. La Mettrie can still say that faith speaks of the soul’s immortality or higher destiny, but philosophy, as empirical physiology, sees cognition arising from sensibility.

⸻

7. Memory, imagination, dreams, and delirium

La Mettrie’s cognitive architecture is materialist and associationist.

Memory is explained mechanically through traces or impressions in the brain. In the Traité, he says that the cause of memory is wholly mechanical: corporeal impressions or traces of ideas in the brain remain near enough to one another that one trace recalls another. He defines memory as a faculty consisting in durable modifications of the movement of animal spirits caused by vivid or repeated sensory impressions, while imagination recombines sensations recalled by memory into images or tableaux that differ from exact past perception.

This does not make imagination trivial. Imagination is central to poetry, invention, error, dream, madness, and even philosophy. In ordinary waking life, imagination is weaker than external sensation. But in dreams, delirium, and certain pathological states, imagination can produce experiences that function like true perceptions for the subject.

This is important for a philosophy of mind because La Mettrie has a theory of mental presence without external objects. Dreams and delirium show that the mind does not merely copy the world. The sensorium can be activated from within. The same bodily apparatus that receives impressions can also generate quasi-perceptual experience in the absence of external stimulus.

In contemporary terms, La Mettrie is moving toward a naturalistic account of hallucination, dreaming, and imagination as bodily-brain events. He does not need a separate soul to explain inner appearances. The living machine can generate them.

⸻

8. Attention, will, and freedom

La Mettrie’s philosophy of agency is subtle. He is often read as a simple determinist, and there is truth in that. He denies a metaphysical free will detached from bodily organization, passion, temperament, and causes. Yet he also gives attention an important role.

In the Traité, he connects freedom with the faculty of sensing and treats attention as a key act of freedom. He considers the idea that attention can fix the will by its own force, while also acknowledging that the dispute is difficult and cannot be settled by inherited systems.

The resulting picture is not libertarian free will. La Mettrie does not posit an uncaused rational ego. But neither does he reduce agency to external pushing. The organism has internal organization. Attention, habit, desire, imagination, and education can redirect conduct. Freedom, insofar as it exists, is not the independence of soul from body; it is an operation within organized sensibility, a capacity of the living machine to be modified, trained, focused, and moved by reasons, pleasures, images, and needs.

This makes La Mettrie an early theorist of what could be called naturalized agency. The will is not abolished; it is embodied. It is not sovereign; it is a function of the organism.

⸻

9. Passion, appetite, pleasure, and moral psychology

La Mettrie gives the passions and appetites a foundational place. Mind is not first a cold intellect to which passions are later added. The mind is already affective because it is rooted in sensibility.

Food, digestion, climate, sexual constitution, disease, and temperament all affect the soul. He famously remarks that the soul seems to inhabit the stomach when nutrition and digestion transform mood, thought, and character. He also ties differences of mind to climate, nourishment, semen, and bodily constitution.

This produces a naturalized moral psychology. Vice and virtue are not simply free choices of a disembodied soul. They are linked to organization, temperament, education, and circumstances. La Mettrie sometimes uses this to argue for humane treatment: the materialist who understands human beings as machines and animals will not therefore become cruel; rather, recognizing the bodily causes of conduct may reduce vengeance and moral hatred.

This does not make him morally simple. His hedonism, his critique of remorse, and his praise of pleasure have generated controversy. But for philosophy of mind, the central point is clear: moral consciousness is not proof of a supernatural soul. Remorse, conscience, shame, joy, sensuality, and intellectual pleasure are bodily-affective phenomena of organized sensibility.

The Italian reception is especially helpful here because it emphasizes that La Mettrie’s medicine functions as an emancipatory knowledge of bodies: the body is not merely matter in abstraction but flesh capable of pleasure, pain, death, and thought. The formula “I am body and I think” captures the force of this interpretation.

⸻

10. Human beings, animals, and the continuity of minds

La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind is strongly continuous across species.

The Cartesian denial of animal minds is, for him, both empirically implausible and ideologically motivated. In Les animaux plus que machines, he presents the denial of animal soul as a strategy designed to avoid placing humans in the same general class as animals.

In L’Homme machine, he grounds human superiority not in a separate metaphysical soul but in bodily organization, especially brain organization, language, education, and social development. Humans have more complex needs and more complex organs; therefore they have more complex intelligence.

In L’Homme plante, he extends the scale downward. He says one need not invoke a special plant soul to explain plant formation; ancient talk of general and particular souls of plants is treated as chimera, while modern physics invokes God, motion, heat, and organization rather than a separate vegetative soul. Yet he also says that among known beings, the human has the most soul and the plant the least, and he presents nature as a scale with no abrupt leaps.

This is not a simple flattening of all beings into equality. La Mettrie preserves hierarchy, but he makes it gradual rather than dualistic. Human beings are superior to animals in organization, language, society, and acquired capacities, but they are not separated by an ontological abyss. Plants are much less animated, but they too belong to nature’s continuum.

So La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind is a graded theory of mentality. Mentality appears wherever organization, motion, need, and sensibility reach an appropriate form. It becomes more intense, flexible, reflective, and social as organization becomes more complex.

⸻

11. God, angels, and the upper scale of minds

This part requires special care. La Mettrie is often read as a radical atheist or as someone whose theological language is merely ironic self-protection. There is evidence for irony in his writings, but reducing every theological passage to disguise would be philosophically careless. The references to God, revelation, faith, and angels play several roles in his thought.

First, La Mettrie sometimes treats God as a possible author of nature and revelation. In L’Homme machine, he argues that if there is a God, God is the author of both nature and revelation, and reason is given to reconcile them. Experience and observation are therefore not impious intrusions into divine matters; they are the proper means by which a rational creature studies nature.

Second, in the Traité de l’âme, he repeatedly marks a boundary between philosophy and faith. Faith alone, he says, can fix belief concerning the nature of the rational soul, immortality, the origin of evil, supernatural assistance, and the future destiny of the soul. Philosophy ends where faith begins. But philosophy can still say, on empirical grounds, that the body appears to think and that the sensitive faculty performs all intellectual functions known to observation.

Third, La Mettrie’s remarks on angels and God are conceptually significant for his philosophy of mind. In Les animaux plus que machines, he cites or reconstructs the idea that the human soul stands to the souls of beasts as the soul of angels stands to the human soul, and as God’s soul stands to that of angels.

That analogy matters. It means that the same scalar logic used to relate plants, animals, and humans can be extended upward. If animals are not mindless machines but lesser organizations of sensibility, then humans may not be the absolute summit of mind. Angelic minds and divine mind become the upper terms of a hierarchy of psychical power. Whether La Mettrie personally believed in those beings is a distinct question. Philosophically, the passage shows that his framework can accommodate a vertical gradation of minds rather than a single human-centered dichotomy.

The mind of God, in this reconstruction, is not treated by La Mettrie as a Cartesian soul lodged inside the world. God appears as possible author, source, highest intelligence, or supreme term in the scale of souls. But empirical philosophy cannot determine God’s mental nature. It can study organized bodies; it cannot claim direct knowledge of divine intellect. Similarly, angelic minds function as possible superior forms of mentality, knowable by theological tradition or analogy rather than by anatomical observation.

This creates a genuine tension. Strict monist materialism seems to leave little room for immaterial angels or a divine mind. Yet La Mettrie’s repeated confession of ignorance about essences and destiny prevents a simple dogmatic exclusion. His philosophy of mind therefore has two levels:

At the empirical level, mind is organized bodily sensibility.

At the theological or analogical level, there may be superior minds—angelic and divine—whose nature philosophy cannot anatomize but which can serve as upper terms in a hierarchy of intelligence.

The most respectful reading is not to decide too quickly that the theological language is either wholly sincere or wholly cynical. It is better to say that La Mettrie’s system naturalizes all minds accessible to medicine and observation, while leaving God and angels at the boundary where empirical philosophy gives way to faith, analogy, and unresolved metaphysical possibility.

⸻

12. La Mettrie and immortality

La Mettrie does not offer a straightforward doctrine of immortality. He denies that philosophy can prove the immortality of a separable rational soul. Yet he also often refuses to claim absolute knowledge of what happens after death.

In L’Homme machine, after insisting that human beings are machines and animals, he warns against dogmatically saying that the machine or animal wholly perishes or assumes another form, because we know nothing with certainty about such ultimate transformations. He invokes metamorphosis as an analogy and says the wise person waits for death without presuming to know what lies beyond.

In the Traité, his more formal position is that faith speaks of immortality, while philosophy confesses ignorance. Philosophy observes dependence of thought on body, brain, sense, memory, age, and organization; it does not thereby gain demonstrative insight into a postmortem spiritual destiny.

So La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind is anti-dualist and anti-metaphysical, but not always crudely annihilationist in tone. His official philosophical claim is: there is no empirical evidence for a separable immortal soul; the observed mind depends on living organization; any claim beyond that belongs to faith or speculation.

⸻

13. The mind as organization, not merely matter

A common misreading says that La Mettrie thinks “matter thinks” in the sense that any lump of matter might think. That is too crude.

His stronger thesis is that organized matter thinks. The difference is decisive. Matter has powers—movement, sensibility, transformability—but mental operations require living organization. The brain, nerves, senses, circulation, and bodily economy form a structure in which sensation becomes memory, imagination, attention, and reasoning.

This is why La Mettrie repeatedly appeals to organization. He does not say that a stone thinks. He says that living matter, arranged in the relevant way, senses and thinks. In L’Homme machine, he correlates differences of genius, stupidity, madness, and animal intelligence with differences in organic arrangement, especially brain organization.

This also explains why his materialism has affinities with several later positions but is identical to none of them.

It resembles identity theory, because mental operations are bodily operations and the body appears to think.

It resembles functionalism, because organization matters more than a mysterious soul-substance.

It resembles embodied cognition, because cognition depends on the whole bodily economy.

It resembles affective neuroscience, because passions, pleasure, pain, and bodily tone are constitutive of mind.

It does not fully resemble modern computational functionalism, because La Mettrie’s mind is not substrate-neutral. It depends on living, sensitive, organic matter.

It is not simple eliminativism, because he does not deny sensation, thought, imagination, or inner life. He denies only their interpretation as operations of a separable immaterial substance.

It is not straightforward panpsychism, because he does not clearly attribute consciousness to all matter equally. His better view is that sensibility is a power of matter that becomes actualized or intensified through organization.

⸻

14. The “machine” as living machine

The phrase “man a machine” should be reconstructed with precision.

A machine, for La Mettrie, is not necessarily a dead artifact. The human machine is self-moving, self-regulating, sensitive, passionate, educable, and capable of pleasure and thought. It is more like a living automaton than a clock. Its “springs” include nerves, fibers, blood, brain, imagination, appetite, habit, and memory.

This is why French and Italian commentators resist reducing him to crude mechanical reductionism. Thomson’s French reading emphasizes the brain-function problem rather than a simplistic machine metaphor. The OpenEdition interpretation argues that the machine metaphor must be transformed to capture material interiority rather than eliminate it. The Italian reception, through Polidori’s edition, emphasizes that the key is not abstract matter but the thinking body: “I am body and I think.”

The machine metaphor therefore has at least four functions.

It denies dualism: no ghostly soul is needed.

It affirms causal intelligibility: mental life has bodily conditions.

It democratizes nature: humans belong to the same continuum as animals.

It secularizes moral psychology: virtue, vice, genius, stupidity, and madness are natural phenomena.

But the metaphor must not be allowed to erase sensibility. La Mettrie’s machine feels.

⸻

15. Language, education, and human superiority

La Mettrie does not reduce human intelligence to raw brain size alone. Human superiority also depends on education, language, sociality, and the multiplication of needs.

In L’Homme plante, he argues that intelligence is relative to movement, need, and organization. Humans have more needs and therefore more mind; they are superior to animals through organization, language, society, laws, and manners.

This gives his philosophy of mind a developmental dimension. The human mind is not simply born complete. It is formed. Sensory experience, education, language, and social habit shape the living machine. In the Traité, the table of contents and examples include cases concerning deafness, blindness, acquired ideas, and sensory deprivation, indicating his interest in how ideas arise from sensory channels and their absence.

So La Mettrie is neither a pure nativist nor a simple environmentalist. Organization supplies capacities; experience develops them. A badly organized brain may not become a genius through education, but without sensation and education, the rational soul has no material with which to operate.

⸻

16. Selfhood and personal identity

La Mettrie does not offer a fully formal theory of personal identity, but one can reconstruct it from his accounts of memory, brain traces, bodily alteration, and disease.

The self is not a metaphysical ego. It is a continuity of organized sensation, memory, affect, and bodily functioning. Because memory depends on brain traces, and because temperament and cognition depend on bodily organization, personal identity is physiologically fragile. Illness, fever, age, madness, sleep, intoxication, and brain injury can alter or suspend the self.

His view implies that the self is a dynamic pattern rather than a substance. The “I” is not an immaterial owner of bodily states; it is the living body’s organized self-relation through sensation, memory, imagination, and desire.

This is one reason his philosophy was scandalous. It threatens not only the immortality of the soul but also the moral and religious image of the person as an autonomous spiritual subject.

⸻

17. Epistemic modesty and anti-systematic system

La Mettrie is often brash, but his deeper epistemology is modest. He repeatedly says that we do not know essences. We do not know what matter is in itself. We do not know what soul is in itself. We do not know the final destiny of the soul. We observe correlations, dependencies, transformations, and effects.

In the Traité, he says philosophy should frankly admit that it does not know the essence of the soul, even while it observes that the body appears to think. He also asks theologians to make imaginable the soul’s essence and postmortem condition, not because he has an alternative dogma ready, but because spiritualist explanations often replace one mystery with another.

The result is a kind of anti-systematic system. La Mettrie builds a coherent monist philosophy of mind, but he does not claim demonstrative metaphysical certainty. He prefers the probable explanation that best fits medicine, anatomy, and experience.

The Italian reception captures this well: La Mettrie refuses totalizing essential explanations; the one-substance thesis is a hypothesis posterior to experiment, and questions such as whether matter is eternal or created, or whether God exists, are not what drives the analysis of the thinking body.

⸻

18. Reconstructed systematic theses

A comprehensive La Mettriean philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as follows.

First, ontological monism. There are not two substances, body and soul. The universe is one substance diversely modified, and human beings are among its most complex known modifications.

Second, empirical method. The mind must be studied through observation, anatomy, medicine, physiology, pathology, and comparison across species, not through abstract metaphysical definitions.

Third, ignorance of essence. We do not know the essence of matter or soul. This ignorance does not license dualism; rather, it blocks dogmatic spiritualist claims that matter cannot think.

Fourth, dynamic matter. Matter is not mere extension. It has motor power and sensibility, or at least the capacity for sensibility when organized.

Fifth, organization. Mental powers depend on bodily organization, especially brain and nervous organization. Differences in genius, madness, stupidity, animal intelligence, and human development are differences of organization, not differences of metaphysical substance.

Sixth, sensibility-first cognition. Sensation or feeling is the root of all mental life. Thought is a faculty of feeling; rationality is sensitive life applied to ideas and reasoning.

Seventh, memory and imagination as material functions. Memory is produced by durable brain traces and the association of impressions; imagination recombines sensory materials and can generate dream or delirious quasi-perceptions.

Eighth, embodied affect. Passions, appetite, pleasure, pain, digestion, sleep, illness, drugs, and climate are not external to mind; they are constitutive conditions of it.

Ninth, naturalized agency. Will and attention are real operations of the organism, but not acts of an immaterial free soul. Freedom is attention and self-modification within the causal life of organized sensibility.

Tenth, animal continuity. Animals are not mindless machines. They have souls or minds insofar as they have organized sensibility. Human beings differ by degree and organization, not by an absolute ontological rupture.

Eleventh, plant lower-limit. Plants do not require a special vegetative soul, but they occupy the lower end of a natural scale of animation, need, motion, and organization.

Twelfth, theological boundary. God, angels, immortality, and the ultimate destiny of the rational soul are not objects of medical anatomy. Faith may speak there; empirical philosophy confesses ignorance. Yet La Mettrie’s own scalar language allows God and angels to be treated as upper terms in a hierarchy of minds.

⸻

19. Main tensions in La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind

The first tension is between materialism and ignorance. La Mettrie says the soul is bodily, but he also says we do not know the essence of matter or soul. This is not a contradiction if his materialism is understood as an inference from observed dependence, not as a complete metaphysical theory of ultimate essence.

The second tension is between mechanism and sensibility. The phrase “machine” suggests dead mechanism, but his actual theory requires sensitive, self-moving, organized matter. The machine metaphor is therefore stretched almost to the point of becoming organic vital materialism.

The third tension is between reduction and preservation. He reduces soul to body, but he preserves interiority, sensation, pleasure, imagination, and affect. His materialism is not a denial of experience; it is a relocation of experience within nature.

The fourth tension is between determinism and ethics. If conduct depends on organization, moral blame seems weakened. La Mettrie accepts much of that implication, but he still cares about happiness, education, humane treatment, and the art of living.

The fifth tension is between radical naturalism and theological language. God and angels are not integrated into his empirical physiology, yet they recur as boundary concepts, analogical upper terms, and objects of faith. A serious reading should preserve that ambiguity rather than erase it.

⸻

20. Final synthesis

La Mettrie’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as a natural history of organized sensibility.

The mind is not an immaterial thing added to the body. Nor is it an illusion. It is the living body sensing, remembering, imagining, desiring, attending, suffering, enjoying, and thinking. The brain is central, but the mind is not separable from the whole bodily economy: nerves, blood, stomach, organs, sleep, illness, climate, age, education, and pleasure all participate in its formation.

Human beings are machines only if “machine” means a living, self-moving, sensitive organization. Animals are not soulless mechanisms; they are lesser centers of organized sensibility. Plants occupy a lower limit of animation. Angels and God, when considered, belong to the upper and theological side of the same scalar imagination of mind: beings whose mentality may exceed ours as ours exceeds that of animals, but whose nature lies beyond empirical physiology.

The most compact formula would be:

For La Mettrie, the soul is organized living sensibility; thought is feeling developed by brain, memory, imagination, attention, and need; human mentality is continuous with animal life and bodily nature; and the highest possible minds—angelic or divine—remain matters of faith, analogy, and epistemic humility rather than anatomical science.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
